facts.text: Seinfeld was created by Jerry Seinfeld and Larry David and premiered on NBC in 1989.
The pilot, originally titled The Seinfeld Chronicles, first aired on July 5, 1989.
Seinfeld is often called 'a show about nothing,' though its creators argued it was really about how comedians find material in everyday life.
The main quartet consists of Jerry, George, Elaine, and Kramer — one of the most iconic ensembles in sitcom history.
George Costanza was heavily based on Larry David's real personality, anxieties, and social disasters.
Cosmo Kramer was inspired by Larry David's real-life neighbor Kenny Kramer.
Elaine Benes became a landmark sitcom character and helped redefine how women were written in TV comedy during the 1990s.
Michael Richards won multiple Emmy Awards for playing the wildly physical and unpredictable Kramer.
Julia Louis-Dreyfus won an Emmy for playing Elaine Benes and later became one of television's most decorated comedy actors.
The series ran for nine seasons and ended on May 14, 1998.
The Seinfeld finale drew more than 76 million viewers, making it one of the most-watched TV finales ever.
Jerry Seinfeld reportedly turned down an enormous payday to make a tenth season because he wanted the show to end on his own terms.
Larry David enforced a famous 'no hugging, no learning' rule so the characters would not become sentimental or moralistic.
The episode 'The Contest' is widely considered one of the smartest and boldest sitcom episodes ever written.
The Chinese Restaurant episode was nearly rejected because almost nothing happened in it — which became exactly why it was memorable.
Seinfeld helped popularize catchphrases and terms like 'yada yada yada,' 'close talker,' 'double-dipper,' and 'shrinkage.'
Festivus, the fake holiday introduced through Frank Costanza, escaped the show and became a real-world pop-culture tradition.
The series was set largely on Manhattan's Upper West Side, even though much of it was filmed in Los Angeles.
Jerry's apartment set is one of the most recognizable interiors in sitcom history, complete with a Superman magnet on the refrigerator.
Jerry Seinfeld is a huge Superman fan, and superhero references appear throughout the show.
Wayne Knight's Newman turned a relatively small role into one of sitcom history's most beloved recurring nemeses.
Larry David left Seinfeld after Season 7 but returned to help write the finale.
Many episodes were built from interlocking storylines that collided perfectly by the final scene, a structure that became a Seinfeld trademark.
The marine biologist episode ends with one of the show's most famous monologues, delivered by George Costanza.
The Puffy Shirt episode became a classic example of how Seinfeld turned minor social misunderstandings into absurd chaos.
The Soup Nazi was based in part on a real New York soup seller known for strict rules and long lines.
The show's observational style had a huge influence on later sitcoms including Curb Your Enthusiasm, It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia, and many single-camera comedies.
Seinfeld won the Emmy for Outstanding Comedy Series in 1993.
The episode 'The Opposite' is often praised as one of the greatest sitcom season finales ever made.
J. Peterman, Puddy, Frank Costanza, and Jackie Chiles became standout supporting characters with instantly recognizable comedic styles.
Jackie Chiles parodied celebrity attorney Johnnie Cochran and became one of the show's most quotable recurring characters.
The show made waiting in line, parking a car, losing a car, returning a jacket, and sharing a marble rye feel like life-or-death comic events.
Seinfeld was initially a modest hit, but it grew into a ratings giant through critical acclaim and word of mouth.
The series generated one of the most lucrative syndication histories in television, earning billions of dollars over time.
Netflix acquired Seinfeld's global streaming rights in the 2020s, introducing the series to another generation of viewers.
Curb Your Enthusiasm later staged a Seinfeld reunion storyline that fans treated as an unofficial return of the cast.
Seinfeld episodes rarely had traditional lessons or warm endings; characters often failed, lied, panicked, or made everything worse.
The final episode put the main characters on trial for their selfish behavior across the entire series, bringing back many past guest characters.
Critics and fans still debate the finale, but the series itself remains one of the most influential comedies ever made.
Even decades later, Seinfeld remains a benchmark for timing, structure, dialogue rhythm, and observational sitcom writing.
The Contest is widely considered one of the smartest and boldest sitcom episodes ever produced.
Its writing and comic restraint helped make it a permanent Seinfeld landmark.
The Contest is one of the signature titles or subjects people mention when discussing Seinfeld.
Any overview of Seinfeld usually makes room for The Contest.
The Contest helped define the tone and reputation of Seinfeld.

The legacy of Seinfeld is hard to explain without mentioning The Contest.
